themeBlurb: Tenses, subject-verb agreement, articles, prepositions, pronouns. Drilled across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze and Editing).

grammar.title: Grammar Foundations
grammar.blurb: The six grammar areas the PSLE tests: tense, subject-verb agreement, articles, prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions.
keyQuestions: Which tense does the sentence need: simple past, past continuous, or past perfect?
Does the verb match a singular or plural subject?
Which preposition naturally follows this verb?

glossary.definition: The form of a verb that shows when an action takes place (past, present, future).
glossary.template: She _____ to school every morning. (walks / walked)

glossary.definition: A singular subject takes a singular verb; a plural subject takes a plural verb.
Words 'a', 'an', and 'the' used before nouns. 'A/an' for non-specific; 'the' for specific.
Words like 'in', 'on', 'at', 'with', 'about' that show relationships between other words.

grammar.learnHTML:
Why grammar matters

Grammar is tested across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ, 10 marks) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze 10 marks + roughly half of the 10-mark Editing component). At least 25 marks ride on grammar precision. A small grammar gap costs more here than anywhere else in the paper.

The six grammar areas

  1. Tense — simple present, present continuous, simple past, past continuous, present perfect, past perfect.
  2. Subject-Verb Agreement (SVA) — singular subjects take singular verbs (The boy walks); plural subjects take plural verbs (The boys walk).
  3. Articles — 'a' / 'an' for non-specific; 'the' for specific. Use 'an' before vowel sounds: an hour, a university.
  4. Prepositions — verb-preposition pairs are fixed: depend on, rely on, succeed in, agree with.
  5. Pronouns — subject (I, he, she, we, they), object (me, him, her, us, them), possessive (my, his, her, our, their).
  6. Conjunctions — joining words: and, but, or, because, although, while, since.

High-frequency tense traps

  • 'Used to' + base verb: I used to play football (not I used to played).
  • Past perfect for the earlier of two past events: By the time the bell rang, the students had already finished their lunch.
  • Conditional 'If' chains: If I were you (not If I was you) — subjunctive after 'If'.
  • 'Have' vs 'Has': She has gone home; They have gone home.

SVA traps

  • Each / Every takes singular: Each of the boys is here.
  • Either / Neither takes singular: Neither of them was on time.
  • One of takes singular: One of the books is missing.
  • Collective nouns (the team, the class) usually take singular in Singaporean English: The team is celebrating.

Verb-preposition pairs to memorise

depend on · rely on · insist on · agree with · agree to · apologise for · complain about · worry about · listen to · wait for · belong to · consist of · suffer from · benefit from · participate in · interfere with · cope with · succeed in · believe in · interfere with · think about · think of
grammar.summaryHTML: Grammar in the PSLE is tested across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ, 10 marks) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze 10 marks + half of the 10-mark Editing). Total: at least 25 marks. The full reference is Grammar_Complete_Reference.md.
pslePrompts: Always identify the tense BEFORE filling the blank.
Re-read the full sentence after filling it — does it flow?
Watch for the 'a/an' trap with silent letters: 'an hour', 'a university'.

questions.question: By the time the bell rang, the students _____ their work.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had finished' is correct because the finishing happened BEFORE another past event (the bell ringing). 'By the time' is a strong signal for past perfect.

questions.question: Neither of the boys _____ available to help.
questions.explanation: 'Neither of' takes a singular verb. 'Neither' is grammatically singular, regardless of the plural noun that follows. So 'was' is correct.

questions.question: She succeeded _____ persuading her parents to let her join the camp.
questions.explanation: The verb 'succeed' takes the preposition 'in'. Other verb-preposition pairs to memorise: depend on, rely on, agree with, apologise for, insist on.
